coredata.dc:title: Portable smartphone platform utilizing AIE-featured carbon dots for multivariate visual detection for Cu2+, Hg2+ and BSA in real samples
coredata.dc:creator: Hao, Yunqi
Ji, Fangyan
Li, Tingting
Tian, Miaomiao
Han, Xu
Chai, Fang
coredata.dc:description: Heavy metals cause serious toxic threats to both environment and human health. The multivariate, instrument-free, portable, and rapid detection strategy is crucial for determination of heavy metals. Herein, aggregation-induced emission (AIE) featured carbon dots (SN-CDs) were fabricated hydrothermally by optimizing co-doping precursors. With bright yellow emission at 560 nm, the SN-CDs were utilized for multivariate sensing Cu2+, Hg2+ and bovine serum albumin (BSA) based on AIE behavior and static quenching effect, with detection limits of 0.46 μmol·L−1, 25.8 nmol·L−1 and 1.52 μmol·L−1. A portable smartphone platform was constructed to enable portable, prompt, and sensitive analysis for Cu2+, Hg2+, and BSA via different strategies in real water and food samples with satisfied recovery. Moreover, a logic gate circuit was designed to provide the possibilities for utilization of intelligent facility. The proposed AIE SN-CDs possessing great contribution in preferable sensing performance, present promising prospects in real-time monitoring of environment and food safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: AIE CDs
Heavy metals
Multivariate detection
Bovine serum albumin
Smartphone platform
Logic gate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Cu2+ and Hg2+ by SN-CDs solution
Detection of BSA by SN-CDs FL quenching sensor
Determination of Cu2+, Hg2+ and BSA in real samples
Detection of Cu2+, Hg2+ and BSA in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Graduate Innovation Fund of Harbin Normal University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors appreciated the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22171060), and Graduate Innovation Fund of Harbin Normal University (HSDSSCX2023-34).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustrative representation of the fabrication of SN-CDs (synthesis and optimisation) and their utility for multivariate sensing Cu2+ (FL enhancement), Hg2+ (FL quenching) and BSA (FL recovery), in food and real water samples by a smartphone colour recognition platform.

(a) TEM image of SN-CDs (inset: HRTEM); (b) XRD of SN-CDs. (c) FTIR spectra of OPD, Tu and SN-CDs. High-resolution XPS of (d) C1s, (e) N1s and (f) S2p. (g) UV–vis absorption, FL excitation and emission of SN-CDs, insert: corresponding images under sunlight (left) and UV lamp (right). (h) The emission with excitation within 390–450 nm. (i) The 3D contour map.

(a) FL intensity ratio I/I0 with different substances. (b) FL emission with Cu2+ (0–100 μmol·L−1). Inset: corresponding images and the linear relationship between I/I0 and concentrations of Cu2+ (1–100 μmol·L−1). (c) FL emission with Hg2+ (0–100 μmol·L−1). Inset: corresponding images and the linear relationship between I/I0 and concentrations of Hg2+ (0.08 μmol·L−1–1.0 μmol·L−1). (d) FL ratio of I/I0 of SN-CDs-Hg2+ with various biomolecules (200 μmol·L−1); inset: corresponding FL spectra and images. (e) FL spectra of SN-CDs-Hg2+ with various concentrations of BSA (0–200 μmol·L−1); inset: corresponding images and the linear relationship between I/I0 and concentration of BSA (2 μmol·L−1–40 μmol·L−1).

(a) Illustration of collecting RGB of fluorescent colour analysing by smartphone with Colour Grab app. (b) Linear plot of between R value and Cu2+ (0.01 μmol·L−1–100 μmol·L−1); (c) Linear plot of the R value versus Hg2+ (0.08 μmol·L−1–50 μmol·L−1); (d) Linear plot of the G value with BSA (2 μmol·L−1–140 μmol·L−1); inset: corresponding colour images.

(a) The histogram of the ratio I/I0 with various concentrations of Cu2+, Hg2+ and BSA. (b) The logic gate scheme (A: Cu2+, B: Hg2+, C: BSA, Y: FL output (enhancement or not), X: FL output (quenching or recovery)); (c) the truth tables of Cu2+, Hg2+, and BSA.
The rate of recovery of Cu2+, Hg2+ and BSA in samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Portable smartphone platform utilizing AIE-featured carbon dots for multivariate visual detection for Cu2+, Hg2+ and BSA in real samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Photochemical Biomaterials and Energy Storage Materials, Heilongjiang Province, Key Laboratory for Photonic and Electronic Bandgap Materials, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Normal University, Harbin 150025, Heilongjiang Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Photochemical Biomaterials and Energy Storage Materials, Heilongjiang Province
Key Laboratory for Photonic and Electronic Bandgap Materials
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Photochemical Biomaterials and Energy Storage Materials, Heilongjiang Province, Key Laboratory for Photonic and Electronic Bandgap Materials, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Normal University, Harbin 150025, Heilongjiang Province, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Computer Science and Information Engineering, Harbin Normal University, Harbin, Heilongjiang 150025, Heilongjiang Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Computer Science and Information Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Computer Science and Information Engineering, Harbin Normal University, Harbin, Heilongjiang 150025, Heilongjiang Province, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heavy metals cause serious toxic threats to both environment and human health. The multivariate, instrument-free, portable, and rapid detection strategy is crucial for determination of heavy metals. Herein, aggregation-induced emission (AIE) featured carbon dots (SN-CDs) were fabricated hydrothermally by optimizing co-doping precursors. With bright yellow emission at 560 nm, the SN-CDs were utilized for multivariate sensing Cu2+, Hg2+ and bovine serum albumin (BSA) based on AIE behavior and static quenching effect, with detection limits of 0.46 μmol·L−1, 25.8 nmol·L−1 and 1.52 μmol·L−1. A portable smartphone platform was constructed to enable portable, prompt, and sensitive analysis for Cu2+, Hg2+, and BSA via different strategies in real water and food samples with satisfied recovery. Moreover, a logic gate circuit was designed to provide the possibilities for utilization of intelligent facility. The proposed AIE SN-CDs possessing great contribution in preferable sensing performance, present promising prospects in real-time monitoring of environment and food safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: I have shared the link to my data/code at the attach file step

ce:section.ce:para: Highly toxic heavy metals pose enormous threats to the environment as well as human health, for their high toxicity, minimal biodegradability, and accumulation in food chain (Reshmy et al., 2022). Among them, mercury ion (Hg2+) is one of the most hazardous heavy metals, which can cause irreversible injury to the reproductive system and central nervous system, even at trace doses (Chen et al., 2021). Cu is considered essential to reach certain concentrations in human body, but excessive Cu2+ has potential toxicity (Bhatt et al., 2020), leading to coronary heart disease and neurodegenerative disorders such as Alzheimer's (Chopra et al., 2022). Among organisms susceptible to incorporating heavy metal contaminants into their organisms, seafood represents a major deal due to its ingestion by the population via dietary intake, while water is essential for life, also becomes the main channel of intake (Castro et al., 2022). Therefore, it is crucial to monitor Hg2+ and Cu2+ in food and environment water to avoid the public health risk. Thus, very rigorous criteria have been stipulated by the World Health Organization (WHO) and US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA). They prescribed a maximum permissible amount in drinkable water of Hg2+ (30 nmol·L−1, 10 nmol·L−1), Cu2+ (31.5 μmol·L−1, 20 μmol·L−1), respectively (Pudza et al., 2020). Furthermore, as an important allergenic protein, BSA is a prominent indicator of food safety and was considered a standard protein in the design of new immunochemical tests. Consequently, the quantitative determination of BSA is crucial for food security and life sciences. The detection of BSA has attracted more attention due to its application in the area of biochemical, food, and immunological science (Esfahlan et al., 2019).

In fact, traditional techniques such as atomic absorption spectroscopy (Batool et al., 2020), surface-enhanced Raman scattering, electrochemical analysis (Butmee et al., 2021), and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry were restricted in their real on-site utility for suffering from many shortage, including high cost, complex professional operation, and time-consuming (Song et al., 2023). Further, to ensure reliable and precise analysis, removing or minimising the matrix effect before conducting assays or using instrumental tools should also be considered and performed by approaches such as solvent-based extraction techniques such as liquid-liquid extraction, solid-liquid extraction, solid-phase micro-extraction, co-precipitation, adsorption (Steiner et al., 2020; Nichols and Geddes, 2021). The most appropriate clean-up should be taken based on real cases (Rezaee et al., 2010). Therefore, it is urgent to design a simple, high-precision as well as multivariate detection method. Novel-type optical and electronic probes presented significant advantages in speed and simplicity. Among them, the fluorescent method has attracted increasing attention by applying various potent and easy-modulating fluorescent nano-materials, including quantum dots, Au nanoclusters, and carbon dots (CDs) (Liu et al., 2020). As a promising fluorescent nanomaterial, CDs possessed the advantages of easy synthesis and surface decoration, excellent photo-stability and water-solubility, as well as modifiable fluorescent colour, low cost, innocuity, and good biocompatibility, notably the aggregation-induced emission (AIE) feature, which make CDs present a superior potential application in sensing and imaging (Li et al., 2021; Xie et al., 2020). However, more methods for sensing heavy metals stemmed from fluorescence (FL) quenching (Ávila et al., 2022) than the reports focusing on FL enhancement (Lv et al., 2019), which caused the visual sensing process to only be carried out under a dark background environment with the assistance of an additional UV lamp. Therefore, a more effective detection strategy was urgently necessary to develop and fabricate, with excellent sensitivity, selectivity, intuitiveness, and flexibility.

AIE was discovered by Tang et al. in 2001 (Luo et al., 2001), which exhibits diametrically opposed aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ). AIE characteristics have been praised and utilised in widespread research areas since peculiar AIE emission induced by intramolecular rotation restriction of the aggregates and the radiation channel being opened (Xie et al., 2020; Yan et al., 2021; Hong et al., 2009). Such a unique feature makes the AIE-active materials notably effective in sensing, achieving high-contrast recognition, and imaging (Jiang et al., 2019), with the advantage of superior user-friendly nature and legible readout, avoiding the ACQ problem (Arshad et al., 2021). Lv et al. synthesised yellow fluorescent OPD-CDs and used them to detect Cu2+ with high sensitivity based on AIE enhancement properties (Lv et al., 2019). Wang et al. developed colorimetric fluorescent paper for detecting Cd2+ via the AIE strategy; and the quantitative sensing in rice samples with assistant of smartphone platform (Wang et al., 2020). AIE-featured CDs integrate the merits of easy fabrication, low cost, and excellent biocompatibility with the specific AIE feature, and overcome their ACQ induced by strong π-π interactions in their graphitizing cores, which enable their potential outstanding utility in sensing and bio-imaging (Yang et al., 2019).

Further, multivariate detection as a promising efficient strategy was expected to be developed for sensing multiple heavy metals simultaneously with the merits of high efficiency, cost-effectiveness, and reliability. The design of multifunctional probes is urgently requested for high-throughput testing. However, the great challenge of assuring high selectivity for multivariate detection should be conquered to ensure separate recognition in multiple detection. If different targets can be recognised by a probe with different response results simultaneously without mutual interference, it can greatly improve detection efficiency with eliminating the cost environmental governance and bio-sensing extremely.

Herein, typical AIE-featured SN-CDs were fabricated and achieved multivariate detection for Cu2+, Hg2+, and BSA. By modulating the ratio of precursors (o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and thiourea (Tu)) and reaction conditions, the optimised AIE-featured yellow emissive SN-CDs were obtained with a quantum yield (QY) of 10.9 %. The SN-CDs exhibited reliable sensing performance for Cu2+ via the characteristic AIE mechanism, and a sensitive quenching response towards Hg2+ based on the static quenching effect (SQE). Sucessively, the fluorescence eliminated by Hg2+ can be recovered by BSA, which enabled a consecutive “on–off-on” detection strategy for Hg2+ and BSA. The quantitative analysis of Cu2+, Hg2+, and BSA in real samples was accomplished by testing the heavy metal residue in food and real environmental water samples. Besides, the sensing response mimics the logic functions “PASS” and “IMPLICATION” were explored for the construction of molecular logic gates to promote understanding of the detection process.

The fabrication of SN-CDs was performed and optimised for reaction conditions including temperature, reaction time, and ratio of precursors (Fig. S1). Briefly, OPD and Tu were dispersed in 10 mL of ultrapure water with a molar ratio of 1: 2, 1: 1.5, 1: 1, 1.5: 1, 2: 1, respectively, and then heated at 180 °C in a Teflon autoclave for 4 h. After being cooled to ambient temperature naturally, a chrome-yellow product was collected after purifying through a 0.22 μm filter membrane and dialyzing in a 1000 Da dialysis bag consecutively. Finally, the SN-CDs powder was collected by lyophilizer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ and Hg2+ by SN-CDs solution
ce:section.ce:para: By dispersing powder in water (20 mg/mL) and diluting it 120 times, the SN-CDs probe was created. For investigating the selectivity, relevant cations (100 μmol·L−1 of Hg2+, Ba2+, Ag+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Fe2+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, and Pb2+) and 19 biomolecules, including (200 μmol·L−1 of BSA, ascorbic acid (AA), l-glutamic acid (GA), l-histidine (His), l-cysteine (Cys), l-glutathione (GSH), l-tryptophan (Trp), glucose (Glu), l-methionine (Met), dopamine (DA), l-phenylalanine (Phe), l-citrulline (Cit), l-lysine (Lys), l-leucine (Leu), l-serine (Ser), Urea, uric acid (UA), l-threonine (Thr) and l-aspartic acid (Asp)) were picked for evaluating their interference. To estimate the sensitivity, various concentrations of Hg2+ and Cu2+ were introduced with the SN-CDs probe (v = 1: 1), and their FL intensity at 560 nm was recorded to evaluate the sensing behavior. All experiments were repeated three times to ensure the accuracy of the measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of BSA by SN-CDs FL quenching sensor
ce:section.ce:para: BSA was successively detected by using the SN-CDs + Hg2+ solution (SN-CDs: Hg2+ (100 μmol·L−1) = 1: 1) as a “turn-on” probe. For estimating the selectivity and sensitivity, 200 μmol·L−1 of various biomolecules (BSA, AA, GA, His, Cys, GSH, Trp, Glu, Met, DA, Phe, Cit, Lys, Leu, Ser, Urea, UA, Thr, Asp, lysozyme (LZ), trypsin (Try), ovalbumin (OVA), collagen (Col), zein, and bovine haemoglobin (BHb)) or BSA solution (0, 2, 6, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, 140, 160, 180, and 200 μmol·L−1) were reacted with probe (1: 1). The detection results can be evaluated by analyzing the FL intensity of SN-CDs at 560 nm.

The detection supported by a smartphone (vivo y73s) with a colour-recognising app (Colour Grab, Loomatix) was established for multivariate sensing for Cu2+, Hg2+, and BSA. The RGB values of the FL colour of SN-CDs were collected under a 365 nm UV lamp and then transferred to readout the results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cu2+, Hg2+ and BSA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Tap water (our laboratory), sea water (Yellow Sea, Shandong Province), fish (crucian carp), and shrimp (local market), and milk (supermarket) were collected and served as the actual specimens. Pretreatment of fish and shrimp was performed according to the reported approach with a slight improvement (Supporting information) (Tsiasioti and Tzanavaras, 2021). All real samples were filtered by a 0.22 μm microporous membrane first, and then spiked with various concentrations of Cu2+, Hg2+, and BSA for practical testing successively.

To obtain typical featured AIE CDs, the synthesis was optimised and modulated by tuning the molar ratio of precursors, reaction time, and temperature, respectively. The optimisation of synthesis emphasised two main strategies: regulating the chemical composition and structure of SN-CDs by doping N and S as dopants and adjusting the reaction conditions, including temperature (120 °C–200 °C), reaction time (2–8 h), and ratio of reactants (OPD: Tu is 1: 2, 1: 1.5, 1: 1, 1.5: 1, 2: 1) (Fig. S1). The FL intensity of SN-CDs (Fig. S1) exhibited the strongest emission at the molar ratio of precursors OPD: Tu = 1.5 mM: 1.0 mM, indicating the optimal reactant can be determined at OPD: Tu = 1.5 (Fig. S1 a, b). Consecutively, the investigation results of reaction temperature (Fig. S1c, d) and reaction time (Fig. S1e, f) exhibited the optimal product can be obtained by heating the precursors at 180 °C for 4 h in a 25-mL Teflon autoclave. The optimized SN-CDs were employed in successive detection.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was employed to test the morphology and size of SN-CDs. The SN-CDs (Fig. 2 a) exhibited spherical nanoparticles randomly dispersed with a diameters distribution of 2.25–5.75 nm (Fig. S2). And most of the SN-CDs were mostly (accounting for 72 %) distributed in the range of 3.25–4.25 nm (Fig. S2) narrowly, suggesting the synthetic process has a high level of size selectivity. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) presented well-resolved crystal lattice fringes with the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (inset of Fig. 2a). corresponding to the (100) planes of graphite carbon, indicating their highly crystalline carbon structures (Hao et al., 2022). To further explore the microstructure of SN-CDs, X-ray diffraction (XRD) was investigated and exhibited two broad diffraction peaks around 2θ of 24.2° and 34.5° (Fig. 2b), confirming the sp2 hybrid C-core structure of the CDs (Song et al., 2023). The diffraction peaks at 26.60°, 42.46°, and 43.89° in the XRD patterns are consistent with the crystal planes (005), (100), and (102) of carbon (JCPDS card No. 26–1077), matched the HRTEM result. These results manifested the synthesis of the SN-CDs definitely.

Moreover, the elemental composition and surfacial groups of SN-CDs were identified by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The characteristic CC/CN and CS/CS/CO stretching vibrations were detected at 1502 cm−1 and in the range of 1000–1155 cm−1 (Wu et al., 2019). Compared with the reaction precursors OPD and Tu, a new absorption peak was spotted at 2051 cm−1 (NCS stretching vibration) in the SN-CDs (Vedernikova et al., 2022), demonstrating the successful doping of S and N, and further confirming abundant surficial groups on the CDs. The XPS measurements revealed the electronic state of SN-CDs. Four primary peaks located at 284.8 eV, 400.3 eV, 532.2 eV, and 162.2 eV (Fig. S3) were assigned to C1s, N1s, O1s, and S2p, respectively, with components of C (65.19 %), N (16.69 %), O (13.3 %) and S (4.83 %). The high-resolution C1s (Fig. 2d) exhibited three peaks at 284.73 eV, 285.78 eV, and 288.64 eV, corresponding to CC/CC, CO/CN/CS and CO groups, respectively (Tang et al., 2021), indicating the distribution of abundant carbon-based groups. The N1s (Fig. 2e) was decomposed into two peaks at 399.91 eV and 400.8 eV, assigned to CN and NH groups (Wu et al., 2019), which facilitated the coordination with metals. Two peaks at 162.18 eV and 163.37 eV of S2p (Fig. 2f) are associated with the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 of the CS covalent bond (Zhang et al., 2018), and the existence of CS/C-SOx and C-SOx (x = 2, 3, and 4) species can be confirmed by peaks at 168.76 eV and 169.84 eV (Zhang et al., 2018), which confirmed the doping with S. Therefore, the above data validated the formation of SN-CDs with abundant functional group modifications on the surface, including CO, CS, –NH2 and –OH, which provided effective coordination sites for capturing analytes.

The optical properties of SN-CDs were evaluated by testing their UV–vis absorption and FL spectra. The SN-CDs exhibit a pale yellow colour under sunlight and emit intense yellow emissions under UV lamps (Fig. 2g). UV–vis absorption showed a strong peak at 290 nm (Fig. 2g), which was ascribed to the quintessential absorption of the π-π* transition of the aromatic moieties (Yang et al., 2020). A wide, weak peak located at around 415 nm can be attributed to the n-π* transitions of the functional groups (CO, CN, etc.) on the surface of SN-CDs (Rajendran et al., 2021). Further, SN-CDs exhibit the optimal emission at 560 nm under 415 nm excitation (Fig. 2g), which is consistent with the 3D contour map (Fig. 2i). The emission tested within a series of excitation (390 nm–450 nm) (Fig. 2h), proved the fact of excitations independence, attributed to the uniform size distribution of SN-CDs. Furthermore, the FL lifetime of 18.37 ns and relative QY of 10.9 % (Rhodamine 6G as a reference) were determined, implyed their excellent optical properties.

To ensure the fessibility of SN-CDs, the photostability at different conditions including various pHs, high salt concentration, and UV irradiating time was evaluated (Fig. S5). As a crucial role in practical sensing applications, the effect of pH was first esstimated, with a pH range of 5.0–12.0 (Fig. S5a), the FL intensity exhibited negligible effect, the low FL intensity presented at strong acidity or strong alkaline conditions, attributed to the destruction of the surface groups and the electronic transition of the luminescent centre. Whatever, the SN-CDs could be feasible in large pH scale in normal environmental sensing (Tang et al., 2021). Further, no obvious effect on FL intensity of SN-CDs with high concentrations of NaCl (1.0 M, Fig. S5b) indicated their outstanding tolerance for high-ionic strength solutions. The high resistance to photobleaching was confirmed by no significant alteration under continuous UV irradiation for 100 min (Fig. S5c). All these results confirmed SN-CDs possessed the excellent stability and high environmental tolerance, demonstrating great potential utility in real analysis.

The multivariate sensing performance was explored and investigated by systematically evaluating selectivity and sensitivity. The effects on FL intensity of SN-CDs with relevant metal ions (100 μmol·L−1) and biomolecules (200 μmol·L−1) were estimated to confirm the selectivity first. Notably, Cu2+ triggered a significant enhancement of the FL within all ions (Fig. S6), while Hg2+ induced a sharp quenching of the FL simultaneously. In comparison, the effect of other ions on the FL intensity was almost negligible, indicating high selectivity towards Cu2+ and Hg2+ in SN-CDs. Prominently, the distinguishable ability towards Cu2+ and Hg2+ endows SN-CDs with the capacity for multivariate sensing. To confirm their potential utility in practical samples, their ability to interfere with the coexistence of other interferents was also evaluated with almost no influence on detection, revealing that Hg2+ and Cu2+ can be identified by SN-CDs with an “off” and “on” strategy, respectively. As expected, SN-CDs with a mixture of interferents and Cu2+ (100 μmol·L−1) exhibited little effect, presenting good specificity. These results manifested the excellent anti-interference of SN-CDs. Significantly, the FL intensity of sample SN-CDs + Hg2+ (100 μmol·L−1) presented intense enhancement with BSA, and the evident blue shift of emission and green fluorescence of the sample can be noticed (Fig. 3 d), which enabled us to employ a new practicable approach to determine BSA quantitatively (Fig. 3a).

To further assess the quantitative analysis of Cu2+ and Hg2+, various concentrations of Cu2+ and Hg2+ were employed separately. As the concentration of Cu2+ increased from 0 to 100 μmol·L−1, the FL intensity of SN-CDs enhanced gradually (Fig. 3b), indicating the reliable their dependence relationship corresponding images reflected enhancement of emission progressively (Fig. 3b), which manifested the good dependency. A nice linear relationship (y = 0.0088x + 1.129, R2 = 0.999) was established between FL intensity ratio (I/I0) and Cu2+ concentration (1–100 μmol·L−1) (inset in Fig. 3b), with the LOD of 0.46 μmol·L−1 based on 3σ/k (Zhang et al., 2021). While the emission dramatically slumped with gradual rising Hg2+ (0–100 μmol·L−1) (Fig. 3c), presented intuitionistic FL quenching evolution, corresponding images were in accord with the variation of FL spectra (Fig. 3c). The value of I/I0 showed a good linear response (R2 = 0.999) with Hg2+ concentration (0.08 μmol·L−1–1 μmol·L−1) (inset in Fig. 3c) with the LOD of 25.8 nmol·L−1 (based on 3σ/k), which was lower than the minimum standard of Hg2+ in drinking water stipulated by WHO (30 nmol·L−1) (Pudza et al., 2020), indicating the potential applicable effectiveness of SN-CDs. The comparison of SN-CDs with reported fluorescent sensors was listed in Tables S1 and S2, respectively. Although the detection range of the proposed probe was not the widest, the sensitivity of the proposed sensor was comparative even better than some sensors, demonstrated SN-CDs achieved high sensitivity for multivariate detection of Cu2+ and Hg2+ simultaneously, without mutual competition or interference. These results manifested that the SN-CDs possessed multivariate sensing ability, which proposed a new strategy and approach for monitoring multiple heavy metals effectively.

As previously proven, the BSA can restore the FL of SN-CDs quenched by Hg2+ with the evident FL colour evolution regularly, which motivated us to explore the relationship between SN-CDs + Hg2+ and BSA. The “off–on” sensor SN-CDs + Hg2+ was constructed by optimising their visual colorimetric results with various concentrations of Hg2+. As expected, the introduction of BSA triggered the emission of quenched SN-CDs (Fig. 3d) with a blue shift, accompanied by FL colour evolution from yellow to green. Further studies optimised the detection condition for BSA, and the vital parameters for SN-CDs + Hg2+ sensing BSA were identified. The SN-CDs + Hg2+ (100 μmol·L−1) system can be used as “turn on” probe for sensing BSA via an “off–on” strategy. The selectivity was evaluated by distinguishing within relative biomolecules, including some proteins (LZ, Try, OVA, Col, zein, and BHb), the 90 % of the original FL intensity of the SN-CDs was recovered, with evident visual blue shift and FL colorimetric change from yellow to blue (Fig. S8). In comparison, other samples still remained in quenching states, demonstrating the outstanding specificity for BSA within relevant biomolecules (Fig. S8 and Fig. 3a).

The sensitivity of SN-CDs-Hg2+ towards BSA was investigated by measuring the FL emission with various concentrations of BSA. The FL intensity of SN-CDs-Hg2+ was gradually recovered with the rising concentration of BSA (0–200 μmol·L−1) (Fig. 3e), with blue shifted from 560 nm to 550 nm, caused colour evolution that can be noticed visually (inset of Fig. 3e). The quenched FL of SN-CDs restored by BSA can be attributed to the coordination between Hg2+ and BSA. The corresponding dependence relationship between I/I0 (I and I0 represent the emission intensity of the SN-CDs-Hg2+ system at 560 nm with and without BSA, respectively) and BSA concentration (2–40 μmol·L−1) was obtained (inset in Fig. 3e), the LOD was calculated to be 1.52 μmol·L−1 (based on 3σ/k). Compared with the sensing performances of reported probes, the proposed method exhibited acceptable result (Table S3) (Wang et al., 2015; Chen et al., 2022), the LOD was comparative even better than some reported sensors, suggesting the “off–on” FL sensor can be employed to effectively detect BSA.

Although the FL sensing method can achieve multivariate accurate and reliable analysis results by portable fluorophotometer, the facile, efficient and intuitive visual sensing strategy was eager to be established, prompting their sensing level and efficiency precisely. Thus, the method depends on the colour information (RGB values) assistant by a smartphone, was developed to make sensing more convenient and efficiently (Wang et al., 2019). The FL colour under UV lamp can be captured and transferred to RGB data by a smartphone with a colour recognition app (Colour Grab). The obtained digital colour with a higher precision level was further analysed by exploring the correlation between the RGB values and concentrations. The relationship of R, G, and B values with concentrations of Cu2+, Hg2+, and BSA was evaluated and optimised (Fig. S9-11, Table S4-6) to determine the optimised colour degree by the best-matched combination with concentration of the analyte. Thus, R values were identified as optimal data for sensing Cu2+ and Hg2+, and the G value for recognition of BSA. A good linear correlation (y = 0.879x + 123.017, R2 = 0.999) was obtained by plotting R versus the Cu2+ concentration (0.01–100 μmol·L−1) (Fig. 4
b), with an LOD of 72.9 nmol·L−1. Similarly, the linear correlation of R against concentration of Hg2+ (y = − 2.233x + 143.660, R2 = 0.991) can be established (Fig. 4c) with the LOD calculation of 28.7 nmol·L−1, which was lower than the drinking water criterion of WHO (Pudza et al., 2020), indicating their excellent accuracy and promising potential application. And the relevant linear equation between G value and BSA concentration (Fig. 4d) was expressed as y = 0.649x + 45.064 (R2 = 0.995) with LOD of 0.51 μmol·L−1. The above results demonstrated the achievement of multivariate detection of Cu2+, Hg2+, and BSA, and the great potential application of SN-CDs via portable smartphone-assisted analysis on-site. Though multiple-functional detection can be achieved, the limits of this sensing system should also be noticed. The linear relationship scales of Hg2+ (0.08 µM–1 µM) and BSA (2 µM–40 µM) are not very wide scales, which is conducive to detection in practical application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+, Hg2+ and BSA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the accuracy and feasibility of SN-CDs, the detection of Cu2+, Hg2+, and BSA in food and real water samples was carried out. Usually, the matrix effect should be considered in the real sample detection, because the matrix compounds can interfere with the accurate quantification and identification of target species. It is crucial to remove or minimize the presence of matrix compounds to ensure reliable and precise analysis. In this work, the matrix effect in the detection can be ignored because the same solvent used in the extraction process is nitric acid, which is the same as the solution of Hg2+. As summarized in Table 1 , the results obtained were in good agreement with the spiked values, the recovery rates in real samples by monitoring of Cu2+ and Hg2+ were calculated to be 98.75 %–101.92 % and 97.58 %–105.37 % respectively, with the relative standard deviations (RSD) less than 5.50 % (Table 1). And the recovery of BSA in milk samples was 100.29 %–100.58 % with RSD less than 2.86 % (Table 1). These results suggest that matrix effects from the diluted serum did not affect the substantial detection. The RGB analysis of real samples also obtained satisfactory recovery rates, and the same samples detected by ICP-OES were parallel to the data from the smartphone test, with a relative error of less than 2.0 % (Fig. S12 and Table S7). These satisfactory recovery results manifested the proposed method for the quantitative determination of Cu2+, Hg2+, and BSA in real samples with highly accuracy and reliability, which further proved the excellent precision and environmental tolerance of the SN-CDs probe.

Since the superiority of portable, sensitive, and selective, presented SN-CDs can fulfil multivariate sensing effectively, to understand and apply the detection process clearly, the molecular logic gate was constructed. Combining visual FL sensing with intelligent molecular computing and simulating the logic functions, the semi-quantitative analysis of Cu2+, Hg2+, and BSA was constructed (Liu et al., 2016). The Cu2+ concentration (0–100 μmol·L−1) was defined as input signal “A”, Hg2+ (0–100 μmol·L−1) set as input “B”, and BSA (0–200 μmol·L−1) as input “C”, respectively. The inputs “1” denote present and “0” mean absent. The I/I0 below and above the threshold value of 1.32, 0.26, and 1.53 were set as 0 and 1 for Cu2+, Hg2+, and BSA, respectively, and their concentrations corresponded to 20 μmol·L−1, 40 μmol·L−1, and 40 μmol·L−1 (Fig. S13). Fig. 5c provides the corresponding truth table with outputs (0) and (1) indicating no FL and FL. When input Cu2+ was less than 20 μmol·L−1 (Fig. 5c), the output Y was (0), meaning that less Cu2+ could not trigger the enhancement of SN-CDs. While more than 20 μmol·L−1 Cu2+ can trigger the boost of FL intensity, output is (1). Since the emission of SN-CDs can be eliminated by Hg2+, while BSA can restore FL, the output X was (0) when input B was present and input C was absent. But the resultant output X was always kept (1) when input C was present, which manifested the “off–on” strategy between Hg2+ and BSA and SN-CDs. In brief, this logic gate devised based on concentration and FL variation was projected for semi-quantitative determination of Cu2+, Hg2+, and BSA, providing a good approach for various logic gates to integrate more logic operations.

The possible mechanism of the sensing platform for Cu2+, Hg2+, and BSA was investigated to understand the strategy. Generally, the interaction with the target analyte was achieved by coordination between their surface groups and analytes, which caused FL quenching or enhancement (Batool et al., 2020). The interaction between Cu2+ and SN-CDs was studied by measuring the absorbance of SN-CDs with and without Cu2+. The increasing absorbance at 420 nm (Fig. S14) can be observed upon addition of Cu2+, indicating enhancement FL of SN-CDs was not caused by the generation of new substances, which further supported this inference by FTIR (Fig. S15). The increase in absorbance may be attributed to the aggregation of the particles that can both absorb and scatter the incident light source (Sooksin et al., 2018). The aggregation of SN-CDs with Cu2+ was further confirmed by TEM (Fig. S16a) with agglomerated particles, which confirmed the Cu2+ induced the aggregation of SN-CDs particles thereby promoting FL enhancement, and demonstrating the AIE feature of SN-CDs. Zeta potential was employed to confirm the electrostatic affinity of Cu2+ to SN-CDs (Fig. S17). The zeta potential value of SN-CDs changed from − 8.41 mV to 3.98 mV after introducing Cu2+, and the zeta potential transferred to positive for the surface interaction of Cu2+ with SN-CD particles. The FL lifetime (Fig. S18a) of SN-CDs increased from 18.37 ns to 18.69 ns with addition of Cu2+; the enhancement of lifetime suggested no electron recombination pathway change occurred upon the interaction with Cu2+. These results supported the AIE mechanism and indicated the increasing FL emission caused by the restriction of intramolecular motion of the same fluoresce species (Li et al., 2021). Thus, the AIE mechanism can be confirmed firmly by all the evidence obtained above, which achieved the unique sensing for Cu2+.

The “on–off-on” strategy for consecutively sensing Hg2+ and BSA was further explored. The absorption of Hg2+ (Fig. S19) did not overlap with the FL excitation and emission of SN-CDs, excluding the possibility of Förster resonance energy transfer and internal filtering effect (IFE) (Li et al., 2021). The FL lifetime decay of SN-CDs exhibited almost unchanged behaviour with and without Hg2+ (Fig. S18b), indicating no intramolecular charge transfer between SN-CDs and Hg2+, demonstrated the quenching of SN-CDs by Hg2+ was SQE (Li et al., 2021). According to interactions between the functional groups and the analytes, the strategies of the fluorescent probes based on CDs can be mainly divided into supramolecular interaction and chemical reaction (Li et al., 2021). The evident red-shift of absorption (Fig. S14) with Hg2+ and elimination of FTIR (Fig. S15) demonstrated chemical coordination between SN-CDs and Hg2+. The significant aggregation of SN-CDs with Hg2+ can be further proved by TEM (Fig. S16b), manifested the BSA-caused recovery of FL due to its strong chelating complexes with Hg2+ (Amin et al., 2020). The position of absorption of BSA with quenched SN-CDs (Fig. S20) was the same as the absorption of BSA + SN-CDs without Hg2+, indicating the binding capacity between BSA and Hg2+ was greater than that of SN-CDs and Hg2+. In addition, zeta potential (Fig. S17) and FTIR spectra (Fig. S21) showed that the effect of Hg2+ on SN-CDs was eliminated after the introduction of BSA, indicating the capture performance of BSA for Hg2+.

Based on the above analysis, the characteristic AIE feature of SN-CDs supports sensing Cu2+; meanwhile, the quenching emission caused by Hg2+ is on account of SQE by forming SN-CDs-Hg2+ complexes via chemical bond coordination, and the “off–on” strategy of SN-CDs + Hg2+ caused by BSA can be assigned to the stronger chelating ability of BSA towards Hg2+. Therefore, the SN-CDs achieved multivariate detection via different signals as read out ingeniously.

In this work, SN-CDs with bright yellow emission were synthesised and optimised by hydrothermal method, and fabricated a multivariate probe for Cu2+, Hg2+, and BSA with the merits of sensitivity, selectivity, no mutual interference. The FL signal of SN-CDs significantly is enhanced by Cu2+ based on AIE, Hg2+ can significantly quench the FL of SN-CDs via the SQE, and BSA can restore the quenched FL of SN-CDs by Hg2+ owing to the stronger chelating ability between BSA and Hg2+. The low detection limit (Cu2+: 0.46 μmol·L−1, Hg2+: 25.8 nmol·L−1, BSA: 1.52 μmol·L−1), and the accuracy enables reliable sensing and monitoring of Cu2+, Hg2+, and BSA in environmental real samples and food. In addition, based on the designed FL sensing system, a portable smartphone sensing platform was constructed with LODs of 72.9 nmol·L−1, 28.7 nmol·L−1, and 0.51 μmol·L−1 for Cu2+, Hg2+, and BSA, respectively. Notably, according to the discoloration of the sensing system induced by different analytes and according to different output definitions, multi-level fluorescent logic gates and logic circuits were constructed, provides potential application of the nano-sensor in the field of intelligent devices.

Yunqi Hao: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Fangyan Ji: Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Tingting Li: Investigation, Data curation. Miaomiao Tian: Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Xu Han: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Fang Chai: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciated the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22171060), and Graduate Innovation Fund of Harbin Normal University (HSDSSCX2023-34).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2024.138843.
